message:
docs: starlight site mirroring the drawmode/querymode pattern

docs-site/ is an Astro + Starlight setup with pages for Overview,
Quickstart, API Reference, Backends, Benchmarks, Architecture, and
Caveats. Deployment wires to GitHub Pages at
https://teamchong.github.io/pii-wasm via .github/workflows/docs.yml;
triggers on pushes that touch docs-site/.

Separate from the in-repo engineering docs at docs/ (roadmap,
benchmarks measurements, stage3 gate). The engineering notes stay
where they are; docs-site/ is the user-facing landing + reference.

Build confirmed locally: 8 pages generated, sitemap + pagefind
search index included. Pinned the zod resolution to 3.25.76 to match
what drawmode/querymode use — Starlight 0.34 + Astro 5.18's sitemap
integration breaks on zod 4.

files.patch:
@@ -0,0 +1,36 @@
+name: Deploy Docs
+
+on:
+  push:
+    branches: [main]
+    paths: [docs-site/**, .github/workflows/docs.yml]
+  workflow_dispatch:
+
+permissions:
+  pages: write
+  id-token: write
+
+concurrency:
+  group: pages
+  cancel-in-progress: false
+
+jobs:
+  deploy:
+    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
+    environment: github-pages
+    steps:
+      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
+
+      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
+        with:
+          node-version: 22
+
+      - run: cd docs-site && npm ci
+
+      - run: cd docs-site && npx astro build
+
+      - uses: actions/upload-pages-artifact@v3
+        with:
+          path: docs-site/dist
+
+      - uses: actions/deploy-pages@v4

@@ -0,0 +1,130 @@
+---
+title: API Reference
+description: Full surface of the pii-wasm public API.
+---
+
+`pii-wasm` has a narrow public surface: one class and a handful of types. Everything else (tokenization, chunking, Viterbi CRF, BIOES merging, backend selection, OPFS caching) is internal.
+
+## `PrivacyFilter.create(options?)`
+
+Static factory. Downloads the model on first call, then caches it via OPFS.
+
+```ts
+static create(options?: CreateOptions): Promise<PrivacyFilter>;
+```
+
+### `CreateOptions`
+
+| field | type | default | notes |
+|---|---|---|---|
+| `modelSource` | `string` | HuggingFace Hub URL | Point at your own mirror (R2/S3/on-prem). |
+| `backend` | `"auto" \| "wasm" \| "webgpu"` | `"auto"` | `"auto"` picks transformers.js (WebGPU when available). |
+| `quantization` | `"int4" \| "int8" \| "fp16"` | `"int8"` | All three map to `model_q4f16.onnx` today. |
+| `onProgress` | `(ev: ProgressEvent) => void` | — | Fires during download + warmup. |
+| `signal` | `AbortSignal` | — | Cancels a slow first-time load. |
+| `markers` | `MarkerStrategy` | `"[<label>]"` | Per-category replacement text. |
+| `enabledCategories` | `SpanLabel[]` | all 8 | Only redact these categories. |
+| `maxChunkTokens` | `number` | `2048` | Chunk size for long inputs. |
+
+## Instance methods
+
+### `redact(text, options?)`
+
+```ts
+redact(text: string, options?: RedactOptions): Promise<RedactResult>;
+```
+
+Returns `{ input, redactedText, spans, summary, containsPii }`. `redactedText` is the input with every detected span replaced by its marker.
+
+### `detect(text, options?)`
+
+```ts
+detect(text: string, options?: RedactOptions): Promise<DetectResult>;
+```
+
+Same inference, no output string built. Use when you want to highlight spans in a UI instead of producing redacted text.
+
+### `redactBatch(inputs, options?)`
+
+```ts
+redactBatch(inputs: readonly string[], options?: RedactOptions): Promise<RedactResult[]>;
+```
+
+Serial processing. For real parallelism, construct multiple `PrivacyFilter` instances.
+
+### `dispose()`
+
+```ts
+dispose(): void;
+```
+
+Releases GPU buffers + WASM memory. Calls after dispose throw.
+
+## Types
+
+### `SpanLabel`
+
+The 8 PII categories emitted by the model:
+
+```ts
+type SpanLabel =
+  | "account_number"   // credit cards, bank accounts, SSN-like identifiers
+  | "private_address"  // street addresses, geographic coordinates
+  | "private_email"    // email addresses
+  | "private_person"   // personal names
+  | "private_phone"    // phone numbers
+  | "private_url"      // URLs pointing to personal accounts/profiles
+  | "private_date"     // dates, birthdays
+  | "secret";          // API keys, passwords, tokens
+```
+
+### `DetectedSpan`
+
+```ts
+interface DetectedSpan {
+  label: SpanLabel;
+  start: number;   // UTF-16 char offset into the original input
+  end: number;     // exclusive
+  text: string;    // input.slice(start, end)
+  marker: string;  // replacement string
+  confidence: number;
+}
+```
+
+### `RedactResult`
+
+```ts
+interface RedactResult {
+  input: string;
+  redactedText: string;
+  spans: readonly DetectedSpan[];
+  summary: Readonly<Partial<Record<SpanLabel, number>>>;
+  containsPii: boolean;
+  warning?: string;  // present only for chunked inputs
+}
+```
+
+### `MarkerStrategy`
+
+Either a static map or a callback:
+
+```ts
+// Static per-category
+const markers = { private_person: "[NAME]", private_email: "[EMAIL]" };
+
+// Dynamic per-span (indexed)
+const markers = (span, i) => `[${span.label.toUpperCase()}_${i}]`;
+```
+
+Return `null` to leave a given span unredacted.
+
+### `ProgressEvent`
+
+```ts
+type ProgressEvent =
+  | { stage: "download"; loaded: number; total: number; url: string }
+  | { stage: "cache-hit"; total: number }
+  | { stage: "compile"; backend: "webgpu" | "wasm" }
+  | { stage: "warmup" }
+  | { stage: "ready" };
+```

@@ -0,0 +1,100 @@
+---
+title: Architecture
+description: How pii-wasm turns text into redacted output, from tokenizer to spans.
+---
+
+## Pipeline
+
+```
+ input text
+    │
+    ▼
+ ┌─────────────────────┐
+ │ Tokenizer           │   @huggingface/transformers AutoTokenizer
+ │   (shared JS)       │   + byte-level BPE reverse map for offsets
+ └──────────┬──────────┘
+            │ tokenIds, attentionMask, tokenToCharOffset
+            ▼
+ ┌─────────────────────┐
+ │ Chunking            │   Sliding window if > maxChunkTokens (2048)
+ └──────────┬──────────┘
+            │ Chunk[]
+            ▼
+ ┌─────────────────────┐
+ │ Backend.forward()   │   One of:
+ │                     │     transformers.js (ORT-Web WebGPU)
+ │                     │     webgpu (our WGSL)
+ │                     │     wasm   (our Zig + SIMD128)
+ └──────────┬──────────┘
+            │ Float32Array [T, 33]  (background + 8 labels × BIOES)
+            ▼
+ ┌─────────────────────┐
+ │ Viterbi CRF         │   Matches upstream calibration biases
+ │                     │   (viterbi_calibration.json)
+ └──────────┬──────────┘
+            │ Uint8Array [T]  (best BIOES tag per token)
+            ▼
+ ┌─────────────────────┐
+ │ BIOES → char spans  │   + whitespace trim (matches opf CLI default)
+ └──────────┬──────────┘
+            │ DetectedSpan[]
+            ▼
+ ┌─────────────────────┐
+ │ Redaction applicator│   Replace spans with markers
+ └──────────┬──────────┘
+            │
+            ▼
+       RedactResult
+```
+
+The backend is the only interchangeable piece. Everything else — tokenizer, chunking, Viterbi, span merging, redaction — is shared JS and deterministic.
+
+## Model
+
+`openai/privacy-filter` (released 2026-04-20):
+
+- Bidirectional token classifier derived from `gpt-oss`.
+- 1.5B total / 50M active (Mixture-of-Experts: 128 experts, top-4 routing).
+- 8 transformer blocks, pre-norm, grouped-query attention (14 query / 2 KV heads, `head_dim = 64`), sparse MoE FFN.
+- RoPE with YARN scaling (`factor = 32`).
+- Sliding window attention with sinks (window = 128).
+- Head: 33 classes (`O` + 8 labels × `B/I/E/S`).
+
+Full architecture reference: [HuggingFace model card](https://huggingface.co/openai/privacy-filter) · [Model card PDF](https://cdn.openai.com/pdf/c66281ed-b638-456a-8ce1-97e9f5264a90/OpenAI-Privacy-Filter-Model-Card.pdf)
+
+## Weights
+
+We read OpenAI's upstream `onnx/model_q4f16.onnx` + `.onnx_data` directly. No conversion step; both the Zig WASM backend and the WGSL backend consume the same ~772 MB download. Tensors are reshaped at load time:
+
+- `int4` quantized weights pass through packed
+- fp16 scales / biases pass through
+- f32 router scales + biases down-converted to fp16 to match kernel signatures
+- Synthetic zero-point buffer (0x88 = signed-int4 centered on 0) generated for the QMoE experts
+
+## Backend internals
+
+### transformers.js (Stage 0)
+
+Thin wrapper around `AutoModelForTokenClassification`. We bypass `pipeline('token-classification')` because it runs its own argmax / BIOES decode; we need raw logits.
+
+### Zig + WASM (Stage 1)
+
+Hand-written kernels in `src/zig/wasm_exports.zig`, compiled with `-target wasm32-freestanding -mcpu=generic+simd128+relaxed_simd -O ReleaseFast`. `dist/pii.wasm` is ~25 KB, inlined into the JS bundle.
+
+Kernels: embed lookup, RMSNorm, int4 matmul (three X×Out type variants), RoPE, banded attention with sinks, swiGLU with clamp, QMoE dispatch, scatter-add, cast, softmax, topK.
+
+### WGSL (Stage 2)
+
+14 compute shaders in `src/js/backends/webgpu.ts`. Highlights:
+
+- `MATMUL_INT4_FP16_F16`: fp16 input → fp16 output, with **8-nibble/word unpack** (one u32 load dispatches 8 FMAs). Used for Q/K/V/O projections.
+- `BANDED_ATTENTION`: one workgroup per `(t_query, h_query)`, 64 threads sharing scores/softmax scratch in workgroup memory, one thread per head_dim lane for AV combine.
+- `QMOE_GATE_UP` + `SWIGLU_CLAMP` + `QMOE_DOWN_SCATTER`: token-major MoE (one workgroup per `(token, k_pick)`), atomic CAS on f32-as-u32 bits for the scatter-add. No CPU readback needed.
+
+## Design invariants
+
+1. **Public API is narrow** — only `PrivacyFilter.create`, `redact`, `detect`, `redactBatch`, `dispose`, and the 7 exported types.
+2. **Backends are interchangeable** — all three produce byte-identical spans.
+3. **Weights live outside the npm bundle** — package ships ~1.8 MB of JS; model is fetched on first use and persisted via OPFS.
+4. **No server-side fallback** — if inference can't run locally, we error out. We don't silently call home.
+5. **Apache 2.0** — matches the upstream model.

@@ -0,0 +1,68 @@
+---
+title: Backends
+description: Three execution engines behind the same public API.
+---
+
+`pii-wasm` ships three interchangeable backends. The public API (`PrivacyFilter.create`, `redact`, `detect`) is identical across all three — the backend is an implementation detail picked at construction.
+
+| Backend | Device | Compute | When to use |
+|---|---|---|---|
+| `auto` (default) | WebGPU via ORT-Web; CPU in Node | transformers.js | Drop-in, works everywhere |
+| `webgpu` | WebGPU with `shader-f16` | Hand-tuned WGSL | **Fastest.** Modern Chromium, recent Safari, Firefox with WebGPU enabled |
+| `wasm` | CPU | Zig + SIMD128 | Universal fallback. Only working CPU path for this model |
+
+## Backend decision tree
+
+```
+ ┌──────────────────────────┐
+ │ Does target have WebGPU? │
+ │   (navigator.gpu +       │
+ │    shader-f16 feature)   │
+ └────────────┬─────────────┘
+              │
+       ┌──────┴──────┐
+       │             │
+      yes            no
+       │             │
+       ▼             ▼
+ backend:"webgpu"   backend:"wasm"
+ (fastest)          (only CPU option)
+```
+
+## Compatibility
+
+| | Chromium 147 | Firefox 129 | Safari 19 | Node 22 |
+|---|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
+| `auto` (tjs + WebGPU) | ✅ | ✅ with `dom.webgpu.enabled=true` | ✅ | ⚠️ CPU-only via ORT-Node |
+| `webgpu` | ✅ | ⚠️ shader-f16 preview | ⚠️ shader-f16 preview | ❌ |
+| `wasm` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
+
+Any browser that can run `model_q4f16.onnx` at all can run `backend: "auto"`. For CPU-only environments, only `backend: "wasm"` works — transformers.js fails session creation because ORT-Web's WASM bundle has no `GatherBlockQuantized` or `MatMulNBits` kernel.
+
+## Output parity
+
+All three backends produce **byte-identical spans** on the same input (cross-backend conformance test: `tests/browser/smoke.spec.ts` + `webgpu-smoke.spec.ts`). Logit magnitudes may drift by up to 0.2 RMS due to fp16 rounding differences across 8 layers, but argmax (and hence Viterbi + span decode) is bit-exact.
+
+## Switching backends at runtime
+
+You can't — a `PrivacyFilter` instance is bound to one backend for its lifetime. To switch, dispose and recreate:
+
+```ts
+const wasmFilter = await PrivacyFilter.create({ backend: "wasm" });
+await wasmFilter.redact(text);
+wasmFilter.dispose();
+
+const gpuFilter = await PrivacyFilter.create({ backend: "webgpu" });
+```
+
+Both share the same OPFS cache for the 770 MB model — switching is a ~1.1-second warmup, not a full re-download.
+
+## Memory footprint
+
+| Backend | Resident memory during inference |
+|---|---|
+| `auto` (tjs WebGPU) | ~870 MB JS heap + ~1 GB GPU |
+| `webgpu` | ~30 MB JS heap + ~800 MB GPU |
+| `wasm` | ~800 MB WASM linear memory |
+
+`webgpu` is the most memory-efficient since weights live on-device and JS holds only kernel dispatch state.

@@ -0,0 +1,57 @@
+---
+title: Benchmarks
+description: Measured numbers vs transformers.js on identical inputs.
+---
+
+All numbers measured on an M-series MacBook, Chromium 147 with `--enable-unsafe-webgpu --use-angle=metal`. Median of 5 samples after 2 warmup iterations. Tests in `tests/browser/bench.spec.ts`.
+
+## Per-forward latency
+
+| Input length | tjs (WebGPU) | **pii-wasm (WebGPU)** | speedup | pii-wasm (WASM) |
+|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
+| Short (~12 tokens) | 29 ms | **7 ms** | **4.1×** | 66 ms |
+| Medium (~25 tokens) | 39 ms | **12 ms** | **3.2×** | 158 ms |
+| Long (~80 tokens) | 57 ms | **25 ms** | **2.3×** | 342 ms |
+
+Linear scaling with T — dispatch overhead is negligible at this model size, the WebGPU path is compute-bound and well-utilized.
+
+## Cold start
+
+| | First visit (HF CDN download) | Second visit (cache warm) |
+|---|---:|---:|
+| tjs | 13.3 s | 11.2 s |
+| pii-wasm (WASM) | 14.1 s | 1.8 s |
+| **pii-wasm (WebGPU)** | 14.1 s ¹ | **1.1 s** |
+
+¹ First visit is download-bound (~770 MB from HuggingFace CDN). All backends take similar time on the initial download; the difference is on repeat visits.
+
+**Second-visit cold start — pii-wasm webgpu is 10× faster than transformers.js.** The win comes from OPFS-backed caching: Cache API can't hold the 770 MB `.onnx_data` blob (quota rejects it), so we persist via OPFS where Chromium allocates ~60% of free disk per origin and Safari gives 1 GB.
+
+## Warmup breakdown (cache hit)
+
+From `tests/browser/webgpu-coldstart.spec.ts`, second warmup:
+
+| Step | Cost |
+|---|---:|
+| Adapter + device request | 0.5 s (Metal driver warm-up) |
+| OPFS read (770 MB model) | 0.4 s |
+| ONNX parse | 0.002 s |
+| GPU buffer upload | 0.4 s |
+| Pipeline compile (14 WGSL shaders, parallel) | 0.002 s |
+| **Total** | **1.3 s** |
+
+## Accuracy
+
+On the model's own benchmark (PII-Masking-300k), `openai/privacy-filter` reports **F1 96%** (state-of-the-art). All three pii-wasm backends produce byte-identical span output (`tests/browser/smoke.spec.ts`, `webgpu-smoke.spec.ts`), so the downstream accuracy is the model's.
+
+Our forward-parity test (`tests/browser/webgpu-forward.spec.ts`) confirms argmax agrees on 32/32 tokens of deterministic input, with a logit RMS drift of 0.18 vs the WASM reference — the model's fp16 rounding noise compounding across 8 layers. Argmax (and thus Viterbi + span output) is preserved.
+
+## Reproduce
+
+```sh
+npm install
+npm run build
+npx playwright test bench.spec.ts
+```
+
+First run downloads the 770 MB model to OPFS (~15 seconds), subsequent runs are cache-hit fast.

@@ -0,0 +1,44 @@
+---
+title: Caveats
+description: What pii-wasm does well, and what it doesn't.
+---
+
+## From OpenAI's own model card
+
+> Like all models, Privacy Filter can make mistakes. It may miss uncommon identifiers or ambiguous references, and it can over- or under-redact information when context is limited, especially in shorter text. In high-sensitivity areas such as legal, medical, and financial workflows, human review and domain-specific evaluation and fine-tuning remain important.
+
+`pii-wasm` is a production-grade inference engine for a model that itself is an aid, not an anonymization guarantee. Treat it as such.
+
+## Known model-level gaps
+
+- **No dedicated SSN or passport-number label.** They may be caught by `account_number` (credit cards + bank account numbers) or `secret`, but there's no guarantee. Reported in multiple launch reviews (Decrypt, VentureBeat).
+- **English-first.** The model was trained primarily on English; performance drops on non-English text, non-Latin scripts, and unusual naming patterns. Japanese reaches ~88% F1; other languages are untested.
+- **Short-text edge cases.** Over/under-redaction spikes when there isn't enough context — a bare "Alice" by itself may not be tagged as a name; in longer sentences it will be.
+- **No runtime label policy.** You can't add new categories without fine-tuning. The 8 labels are fixed.
+
+## Implementation-level caveats
+
+- **Browser storage quota.** The 770 MB model is persisted via OPFS. Users with tight storage (Safari's 1 GB origin quota at 77% full, mobile browsers, private-mode tabs) may hit eviction. We fall back to plain fetch if OPFS write fails — no re-download loop, but warmup reverts to ~13 seconds on subsequent visits.
+- **WebGPU availability.** `backend: "webgpu"` requires `shader-f16`. Chromium 147 ships it; Firefox + Safari are still behind a pref. Use `backend: "wasm"` as the universal fallback.
+- **fp16 accumulation drift.** GPU and WASM forward logits RMS-disagree by ~0.18 due to different rounding paths across 8 layers. Argmax is preserved (span output is byte-exact across backends); only the softmax probabilities differ by a few percent in magnitude.
+- **No streaming.** `forward` processes one chunk synchronously; for inputs > `maxChunkTokens` the chunking is invisible to the caller. You get all spans at once, not as they're decoded.
+
+## Not-a-replacement for
+
+- **Compliance review.** Redaction != anonymization. Miranda Bogen (Center for Democracy and Technology) in Bloomberg: *"Foundation models can create privacy violations far beyond what PII filtering can detect."*
+- **Regex-level guarantees.** For known patterns (credit-card Luhn checks, US SSN structure validation), a regex library like `redact-pii` is faster and more deterministic. Run both for belt-and-braces.
+- **Multi-language deployments.** If your users write in Chinese, Arabic, or Hindi, consider Microsoft Presidio or a cloud DLP with native multilingual training.
+
+## When to use `pii-wasm`
+
+- Client-side redaction in browser UIs (form fields, comment boxes, chat prompts before hitting a cloud LLM).
+- Browser-extension "warn before paste" prompts.
+- Offline / air-gapped environments where sending text to a server isn't acceptable.
+- Node pipelines where you want the same model locally without a Python stack.
+
+## Upstream references
+
+- [openai/privacy-filter on HuggingFace](https://huggingface.co/openai/privacy-filter)
+- [Model card PDF](https://cdn.openai.com/pdf/c66281ed-b638-456a-8ce1-97e9f5264a90/OpenAI-Privacy-Filter-Model-Card.pdf)
+- [OpenAI announcement](https://openai.com/index/introducing-openai-privacy-filter/)
+- [HuggingFace transformers docs](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/main/docs/source/en/model_doc/openai_privacy_filter.md)

@@ -0,0 +1,83 @@
+---
+title: pii-wasm
+description: Browser-native PII detection and redaction using openai/privacy-filter.
+template: splash
+hero:
+  tagline: OpenAI's privacy-filter model, running entirely in your browser. No backend, no network calls, no text ever leaves the device.
+  actions:
+    - text: Quickstart
+      link: /pii-wasm/quickstart/
+      icon: right-arrow
+      variant: primary
+    - text: API Reference
+      link: /pii-wasm/api/
+      variant: minimal
+    - text: GitHub
+      link: https://github.com/teamchong/pii-wasm
+      icon: external
+      variant: minimal
+---
+
+import { Card, CardGrid } from "@astrojs/starlight/components";
+
+## Why
+
+OpenAI released [openai/privacy-filter](https://huggingface.co/openai/privacy-filter) on 2026-04-20 — a 1.5B-parameter MoE token classifier (50M active) for PII detection, Apache 2.0. It reaches **96-97% F1 on PII-Masking-300k** and runs on a laptop.
+
+The gap: the official SDK is Python. transformers.js runs it on WebGPU but has no CPU fallback. **`pii-wasm` is the only package that runs this model end-to-end in the browser with both WebGPU and CPU paths.**
+
+## Features
+
+<CardGrid>
+  <Card title="Fully client-side" icon="seti:lock">
+    No backend. No network inference calls. PII text stays on the user's device.
+  </Card>
+  <Card title="Three backends" icon="seti:config">
+    WebGPU (hand-tuned WGSL, fastest), WASM (Zig + SIMD128, universal), or transformers.js (baseline). One public API.
+  </Card>
+  <Card title="Faster than tjs" icon="rocket">
+    2.3–4× faster per forward than transformers.js; 10× faster on repeat-visit cold start.
+  </Card>
+  <Card title="OPFS cache" icon="document">
+    770 MB model downloaded once, persisted across visits. Second launch: 1.1 seconds.
+  </Card>
+  <Card title="Byte-exact decoding" icon="approve-check">
+    Viterbi CRF over BIOES, matching OpenAI's reference decoding.
+  </Card>
+  <Card title="Apache 2.0" icon="open-book">
+    Same license as the model.
+  </Card>
+</CardGrid>
+
+## 30-second example
+
+```ts
+import { PrivacyFilter } from "pii-wasm";
+
+const filter = await PrivacyFilter.create();
+const result = await filter.redact(
+  "Hi, my name is John Smith and my email is john@example.com.",
+);
+
+console.log(result.redactedText);
+// "Hi, my name is [private_person] and my email is [private_email]."
+
+console.log(result.spans);
+// [
+//   { label: "private_person", start: 15, end: 25, text: "John Smith", ... },
+//   { label: "private_email",  start: 43, end: 59, text: "john@example.com", ... },
+// ]
+```
+
+## How it compares
+
+| | tjs (WebGPU) | **pii-wasm (WebGPU)** | pii-wasm (WASM) |
+|---|---:|---:|---:|
+| Forward — short | 29 ms | **7 ms** | 66 ms |
+| Forward — medium | 39 ms | **12 ms** | 158 ms |
+| Forward — long | 57 ms | **25 ms** | 342 ms |
+| Second-visit warmup | 11.2 s | **1.1 s** | 1.8 s |
+
+*M-series MacBook, Chromium 147, median of 5 runs after 2 warmups.*
+
+For **CPU-only environments** (corporate Chrome without WebGPU, Firefox with `dom.webgpu.enabled=false`, server-side Node), pii-wasm is the only working path — transformers.js fails session creation on this model's WASM device due to missing ORT-Web kernels (`GatherBlockQuantized`, `MatMulNBits`).

@@ -0,0 +1,66 @@
+---
+title: Quickstart
+description: Install, detect PII, redact text — in three code blocks.
+---
+
+## Install
+
+```sh
+npm install pii-wasm
+# or: pnpm add / bun add / yarn add
+```
+
+## Redact
+
+```ts
+import { PrivacyFilter } from "pii-wasm";
+
+const filter = await PrivacyFilter.create();
+
+const { redactedText, spans } = await filter.redact(
+  "Please email me at jane.doe@example.com about invoice 4511.",
+);
+// redactedText = "Please email me at [private_email] about invoice [account_number]."
+// spans        = [ { label: "private_email", ... }, { label: "account_number", ... } ]
+```
+
+## Detect (no redaction applied)
+
+```ts
+const { spans, containsPii } = await filter.detect(
+  "Call me at (555) 123-4567.",
+);
+// spans = [ { label: "private_phone", start: 11, end: 25, text: "(555) 123-4567", ... } ]
+// containsPii = true
+```
+
+## Batch
+
+```ts
+const results = await filter.redactBatch([
+  "First message.",
+  "Second message with PII: jane@example.com.",
+]);
+```
+
+## Pick a backend
+
+By default `PrivacyFilter.create()` uses the transformers.js backend with WebGPU (or CPU fallback in Node). To use our own faster kernels:
+
+```ts
+// Fastest: Stage-2 WGSL compute shaders, requires WebGPU + shader-f16.
+const gpu = await PrivacyFilter.create({ backend: "webgpu" });
+
+// Universal: Stage-1 Zig + WASM + SIMD128. Works everywhere.
+const wasm = await PrivacyFilter.create({ backend: "wasm" });
+```
+
+See the [Backends](/pii-wasm/backends/) page for the full compatibility table.
+
+## Dispose when done
+
+```ts
+filter.dispose();
+```
+
+Releases GPU buffers and WASM memory. Safe to call multiple times.
